3: parakalo agapetoi pasan spoude poioumenos graphein hymin peri tes koines soteries anagkaion egesamen grapsai hymin parakalon epagonizesthai te hapax paradotheise tois hagiois pistei: 'I found it necessary to write appealing to you to contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints.' Hapax paradotheise (once for all delivered): the single, unrepeatable handing-over of the faith — paradidomi is the technical tradition-transmission verb (as in 1 Corinthians 11:23, Jude 15:3). The faith is not a developing deposit that each generation revises but a fixed, delivered tradition that needs defending, not augmenting. Epagonizomai (contend earnestly for) is the athletic-contest word applied to doctrinal fidelity — not aggressive attack of opponents but vigorous defense of what was given.
1: Ioudas Iesou Christou doulos adelphos de Iakōbou: 'Jude, a slave of Jesus Christ and brother of James.' The author identifies himself as doulos (slave/servant) of Jesus Christ — the same self-designation Paul uses in Romans 1:1 and Philippians 1:1. As the brother of James, Jude is implicitly identified as a brother of Jesus (Mark 6:3 lists both James and Judas among Jesus's brothers) — but he does not claim that relationship, only the servant-status. Tois... klētois hēgiasmenos en theō patri kai tetērēmenois Iēsou Christō (to those called, beloved in God the Father, and kept for Jesus Christ): three perfect participles describe the elect community — called, loved, kept. Tetērēmenois (kept, guarded) — the same root Jude uses for the false teachers' judgment in Jude 1:6 and the believers' presentation in Jude 1:24, creating a structural contrast: false teachers are kept in darkness (Jude 1:6); believers are kept for Jesus Christ.
2: eleos hymin kai eirēnē kai agapē plēthyntheiē: 'May mercy, peace, and love be multiplied to you.' The tri-fold greeting expands Paul's standard 'grace and peace' (Romans 1:7) with mercy and love. Plēthyntheiē (be multiplied, be made abundant) — the optative form expresses a wish; the same verb appears in the opening of 1 Peter (Jude 1:2) and 2 Peter (Jude 1:2). Jude's triple formula — mercy (eleos), peace (eirēnē), love (agapē) — anticipates the letter's closing: the doxology ascribes glory through Christ (Jude 1:25), and the community's pastoral task includes showing mercy to doubters (Jude 1:22) and mercy mixed with fear (Jude 1:23). The opening wish for mercy is programmatic.
4: pareiseduēsan tines anthrōpoi hoi palai progegrammenoi eis touto to krima: 'Certain people have crept in unnoticed, those who long ago were designated for this condemnation.' Pareisedyō (slip in alongside, infiltrate) — a rare compound suggesting covert, lateral entry; the preposition para-eis- implies they came in by the side without being noticed. Progegrammenoi (written beforehand, designated beforehand) — their condemnation was prophetically anticipated: in the letter, Jude will show that Enoch prophesied about them (Jude 1:14-15). Ton monon despotēn kai kyrion hēmōn Iēsoun Christon arnoumenoi (denying our only Master and Lord Jesus Christ): despotēs (absolute ruler, despot) is the stronger lordship term; its combination with kyrios emphasizes the totality of Christ's authority that the false teachers deny.
5: hypomimnēskein de hymas boulomai... hoti ho kyrios hapax laon ek gēs Aigyptou sōsas to deuteron tous mē pisteusantas apōlesen: 'The Lord, having once saved the people out of Egypt, afterward destroyed those who did not believe.' Hapax (once) — the single decisive Exodus-event serves as the paradigm for grace that does not guarantee perseverance: being saved from Egypt did not prevent those who later rebelled from being destroyed. The typological force is identical to Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 10:1-12 ('they were all baptized into Moses... but with most of them God was not pleased, for they were overthrown in the wilderness'). The variant reading Iēsous (Jesus) rather than ho kyrios (the Lord) — found in some mss (Vaticanus, Sinaiticus) — would make this a Christological pre-existence claim: Jesus destroyed the unbelievers in the wilderness.
6: aggelous te tous mē tērēsantas tēn heautōn archēn alla apolipontas to idion oikētērion eis krisin megalēs hēmeras desmois aidiois hypo zophou tetērēken: 'And angels who did not keep their proper domain but abandoned their own habitation, he has kept in eternal chains under gloomy darkness until the judgment of the great day.' Archēn (authority, domain, beginning, first estate) — the angels abandoned their assigned station in the divine economy. Oikētērion (proper dwelling, habitation) — the cosmic station assigned to the angels; their abandonment of it parallels the false teachers' departure from their apostolic calling. The language draws directly from 1 Enoch Jude 12:4 ('You were in heaven, but its secrets had not yet been revealed to you') and 1 Enoch Jude 10:11-13 (the Watchers bound until the day of judgment). Tetērēken — kept (perfect): the same verb used in Jude 1:1 for believers kept by Jesus Christ; the contrast is pointed.
7: Sodoma kai Gomorra kai hai peri autas poleis ton homoion toutois tropon ekporneusasai kai apelthousai opisō sarkos heteras: 'Sodom and Gomorrah... who gave themselves up to sexual immorality and who went after different flesh.' Ekporneuō (commit sexual immorality intensively, utterly prostitute oneself) — a compound intensifying porneuō; the prefix suggests total commitment to the act. Sarkos heteras (different flesh): heteros marks a qualitative difference rather than mere numerical variation (allos would mean another of the same kind). The 'different flesh' of the Sodom narrative is the angelic flesh of Gen 18-19 — the men of Sodom sought sexual relations with the angelic visitors. Jude uses this as the paired analogue to the angel-human transgression of Jude 1:6: the angels sought human flesh; the Sodomites sought angelic flesh — mirroring transgressions.
8: homoiōs mentoi kai houtoi enypniazomenoi sarka men miainousin kyriotēta de athetousin doxas de blasphēmousin: 'Yet in the same way these dreamers also defile the flesh, reject authority, and blaspheme glorious ones.' Enypniazomenoi (dreaming, being dreamers) — the word implies that the false teachers claim visionary experiences (enypnion = dream, vision) that they use to justify their conduct. The three charges: (1) sarka miainousin (defile/pollute the flesh) — bodily moral contamination; (2) kyriotēta athetousin (reject lordship/authority) — the same kyriotēs as in Jude 1:4 (denying our only Master); (3) doxas blasphēmousin (blaspheme glories/glorious ones) — like 2 Peter 2:10, doxai are probably angelic powers. All three transgressions correspond to the three OT types: the wilderness generation (flesh), the fallen angels (authority), Sodom (glories/celestial beings).
9: ho de Michaēl ho archangelos... ouk etolmēsen krisin epenegkein blasphēmias: 'The archangel Michael, when he was disputing with the devil about Moses's body, did not dare to bring a judgment of slander.' The dispute over Moses's body is from the Assumption of Moses (or Testament of Moses), a Jewish apocalyptic text now only partially preserved. Michael's role as advocate or guardian of Israel (Daniel 10:13, Jude 12:1; 1 Enoch Jude 20:5) brought him into conflict with the adversary (diabolos) over possession of Moses's burial site. Ouk etolmēsen... blasphēmias krisin (did not dare a judgment of slander): the strongest archangel refused to use the blaspheming-accusation method even against Satan. The contrast: the false teachers (who lack even angelic rank) use slander against beings far above them. Epitimēsai soi kyrios (the Lord rebuke you): the formula from Zechariah 3:2.
10: houtoi de hosa men ouk oisin blasphēmousin hosa de physikōs hōs ta aloga zōa epistantai en toutois phtheirontai: 'But these people slander whatever they do not understand, and the very things they understand by instinct—like irrational animals—by these they are destroyed.' The structure is antithetical: (1) what they do not understand — they slander; (2) what they do understand — only by animal instinct, not spiritual discernment. Aloga zōa (irrational/speechless animals) — identical to 2 Peter 2:12's comparison, and drawing on the same Stoic anthropological category of zōon logikon vs. alogon zōon. The bitter irony: the very sphere of their claimed knowledge (sensory experience and instinct) is the vehicle of their destruction. Their 'understanding' is not comprehension but animal sensation.
11: ouai autois hoti tē hodō tou Kain eporeuthēsan kai tē planē tou Balaam misthou exechythēsan kai tē antilogia tou Kore apōlonto: 'Woe to them! For they walked in the way of Cain, and abandoned themselves for the sake of gain to Balaam's error, and perished in Korah's rebellion.' The triple OT woe-oracle uses three paradigmatic rebels: (1) Cain: Gen 4; in Jewish tradition (Philo, Josephus, rabbinic midrash) Cain represents the rejection of divine revelation and the murder of the righteous. (2) Balaam: the mercenary prophet (Num 22-24; Jude 31:16) — representing prophecy-for-profit and leading Israel into idolatry. (3) Korah: Num 16 — rebellion against Mosaic/Aaronic authority. The three represent: moral corruption (Cain), mercenary religion (Balaam), and authority-rejection (Korah) — mirroring the three charges of Jude 1:8.
12: houtoi eisin en tais agapais hymōn spilades syneuōchoumenoi aphobōs: 'These are blemishes at your love feasts, feasting with you without fear.' Agapais (love feasts, agapē meals): the communal meals that preceded or accompanied the eucharist in early Christian assemblies — attested in 1 Corinthians 11:17-34, Acts 2:42-46, 2 Peter 2:13, and the Didache (9-10). Spilades (reefs/blemishes): the word can mean either hidden underwater reefs (nautical, dangerous) or blemishes/spots (cf. 2 Peter 2:13 spiloi kai mōmoi). Both senses work: dangerous hidden obstacles and moral blots. Heautous poimainontes (shepherding themselves): the participle critiques the failure of leadership — shepherds who graze themselves rather than the flock (Ezekiel 34:2-3: 'Woe to the shepherds of Israel who only take care of themselves').
13: asteres planētai: 'wandering stars.' Planētai is the Greek word for the planets (literally wanderers, from planaō, to wander), which unlike the fixed stars followed irregular paths. In 1 Enoch Jude 18:14-16 and Jude 21:3-6, the wandering stars are imprisoned fallen angels whose irregular motion was a sign of their rebellion against their appointed course. Jude's use of the term carries this apocalyptic resonance: the false teachers are like the rebellious angels of 1 Enoch — those who abandoned their station. The five metaphors of Jude 1:12-13 (blemishes, waterless clouds, fruitless trees, wild waves, wandering stars) form a sustained vivid-imagery catalogue that moves from earth (agricultural) through water to sky (cosmic), accumulating rhetorical force.
14: Enōch hektō apo Adam eprophēteusen kai toutois legōn: 'Enoch, the seventh from Adam, prophesied about them, saying.' The identification of Enoch as 'seventh from Adam' (matching 1 Enoch 60:8 exactly) and the direct quotation that follows (Jude 1:14b-15) is drawn from 1 Enoch Jude 1:9 — the only direct NT quotation from 1 Enoch. Hektō apo Adam: the genealogical count (Adam, Seth, Enosh, Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared, Enoch = seven) marks Enoch as an especially significant figure in the antediluvian tradition; Jewish apocalyptic elevated him because of Genesis 5:24 ('Enoch walked with God, and he was not, for God took him'). Jude treats 1 Enoch as prophetic Scripture — a remarkable statement about early Christian canon-consciousness.
15: poiesai krisin kata pantōn kai elenxai pasan psychēn peri pantōn tōn ergōn asebias autōn: 'to execute judgment on all and to convict all the ungodly of all their deeds of ungodliness.' The fourfold panta/pantōn (all/every) in the quotation creates a rhetorically emphatic totality: every person, every act, every ungodly word will be judged. Asebeis (ungodly) and asebeia (ungodliness) appear four times in the quotation's 32 words — the repetition itself is a feature of the 1 Enoch text, emphasizing the specific character of the offense. Sklerōn hōn elalēsan (harsh things they have spoken): the inclusion of speech in the judgment — not only deeds but words — is consistent with Matthew 12:36 ('people will give account for every careless word').
16: gongystai mempsimoiroi kata tas epithymias heautōn poreuomenoi kai to stoma autōn lalei hyperonka: 'These are grumblers, faultfinders, who walk according to their own desires, and their mouth speaks arrogant words.' Gongystai (grumblers): the word echoes the LXX gongysmoi of the wilderness generation (Exodus 16:7-8; Numbers 11:1; Jude 14:27; Jude 17:5) — the grumblers against Moses in the desert. Jude's use sustains the wilderness typology of Jude 1:5. Mempsimoiroi (faultfinders, those who complain about their lot): a rare Greek compound (only here in NT) — memphomenos (blame) + moira (fate, lot) = those who constantly blame their lot, perpetually dissatisfied. Hyperonka (swollen/bombastic words): same as 2 Peter 2:18; the false teachers are verbose and pompous.
17: hymmeis de agapētoi mnēsthēte tōn rhēmatōn tōn proeirēmenōn hypo tōn apostolōn tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou: 'But you, beloved, remember the words spoken before by the apostles of our Lord Jesus Christ.' The pivot from denunciation to exhortation is marked by hymmeis de (but you) — the same contrastive structure as 1 Peter 2:9 ('but you are a chosen people'). Proeirēmenōn (spoken before, previously predicted) — the apostolic word is normative specifically because it was predictive: the scoffers of the last days were announced before they arrived. Jude positions the community as those who can recognize the false teachers because they have been forewarned. The apostolic prediction creates a community of recognition.
18: hoti elegon hymin ep' eschatou chronou esontai empaiktai kata tas heautōn epithymias poreuomenoi tōn asebeion: 'that they said, In the last time there will be scoffers, following their own ungodly passions.' The apostolic prediction (summarized here and in 2 Peter 3:3 in nearly identical terms) likely refers to the teaching of Paul (1 Timothy 4:1: 'in the last times some will depart from the faith'; 2 Timothy 3:1-5: 'in the last days there will come times of difficulty'), Peter (2 Peter 3:3), and perhaps oral tradition. Ep' eschatou chronou (in the last time) — singular, without the article: the eschatological era that has already dawned with the Incarnation, death, and resurrection of Christ. The community is living in the last time; the predicted scoffers have arrived.
19: houtoi eisin hoi apodiorizontes psychikoi pneuma mē echontes: 'These are those who cause divisions, worldly people, devoid of the Spirit.' Apodiorizontes (those who make divisions, who draw boundary-lines separating): a compound verb meaning to divide by drawing a line (horizō) — possibly a technical term for factional separating in the community. Psychikoi (soulish/natural/worldly): the critical Pauline distinction of 1 Corinthians 2:14-15 — the psychikos anthrōpos (natural/soulish person) cannot receive the things of the Spirit; the pneumatikos (spiritual person) discerns them. The false teachers who claim superior spiritual insight (enypniazomenoi, Jude 1:8) are in fact defined by the absence of the Spirit — the very thing they claim to have. Pneuma mē echontes (not having the Spirit): this is the decisive marker separating true and false spirituality in Johannine (1 John 4:1-6) and Pauline (Romans 8:9) terms.
20: hymeis de agapētoi tē hagiōtatē hymōn pistei epoikodominountes heautous en pneumati hagiō proseuchomenoi: 'But you, beloved, building yourselves up in your most holy faith, praying in the Holy Spirit.' The counterpoint to Jude 1:19's Spirit-less false teachers: the community is defined by Spirit-animated prayer. Epoikodomiō (build up upon, edify) — the architectural image of a community being constructed on a foundation (the faith of Jude 1:3). Hagiōtatē pistei (most holy faith): the superlative hagiōtatē (holiest, most sacred) — only here in the NT; it intensifies the special character of the delivered faith. En pneumati hagiō proseuchomenoi (praying in the Holy Spirit): cf. Ephesians 6:18; the Spirit-given prayer is both the mark of the genuine community and the mechanism of their edification.
21: heautous en agapē theou tērēsate prosdechomenoi to eleos tou kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou eis zōēn aiōnion: 'Keep yourselves in the love of God, waiting for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ to eternal life.' Tērēsate (keep, guard) — the same root as Jude 1:1 (believers kept by Jesus Christ), Jude 1:6 (angels not keeping their station), and Jude 1:24 (God keeping us from stumbling). The keeping is simultaneously divine and human — God keeps believers (Jude 1:1, 24) and believers keep themselves in God's love. This is not self-salvation but the responsive faithfulness that covenant relationship requires. Prosdechomenoi to eleos (waiting for/expecting the mercy): the mercy that is expected is the mercy of Christ's parousia — Christ coming to bring his people into eternal life.
22: kai hous men eleate diakrinomenous: 'And have mercy on those who are wavering.' Diakrinomenoi (wavering, doubting, disputing) — the participle from diakrinō, which can mean to judge/distinguish (active), to doubt/waver (middle/passive), or to dispute. Here the community is to exercise graduated pastoral care: those who are wavering or doubting are to receive mercy (eleō, show mercy), not condemnation. The pastoral wisdom recognizes that the false teaching has produced a spectrum of responses: some fully committed to the error, others attracted but uncertain, others actively resistant. The graduated response (Jude 1:22-23) requires discernment rather than a single approach to all affected people.
23: hous de sōzete ek pyros harpazentes: 'save others by snatching them out of the fire.' Harpazō (seize suddenly, snatch) — a word used for violent seizure (John 10:12: the wolf snatches the sheep; Acts 8:39: the Spirit caught away Philip; 1 Thessalonians 4:17: believers caught up to meet the Lord). The urgency of the rescue image: as one snatches a burning object from a fire, some in the community require immediate, decisive intervention. Misountes kai ton apo tēs sarkos espilōmenon chitōna (hating even the clothing stained by the flesh): chitōn (tunic, undergarment) — the garment stained by the corrupted flesh is a vivid image for the moral contamination of the false teaching. The care and mercy required does not involve tolerating or touching the sin itself.
24: tō de dynamenō phylaxai hymas aptaistous kai stēsai katenōpion tēs doxēs autou amōmous en agalliasei: 'Now to him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy.' Aptaistous (without stumbling, lit. without striking the foot): hapax NT; a single compound word for the condition of not tripping — the negative image of ptaiō (stumble). Amōmous (blameless, without blemish) — the sacrificial term: the unblemished sacrifice (Hebrews 9:14: Christ offered himself amōmos). The community presented before God is described in sacrificial purity language — they are the living sacrifice, the holy and acceptable offering. Katenōpion tēs doxēs autou (before the presence of his glory): the divine glory-presence (Shekinah) before which no blemished offering could stand (Leviticus 22:20).
25: monō theō sōtēri hēmōn dia Iēsou Christou tou kyriou hēmōn doxa megalōsynē kratos kai exousia pro pantos tou aiōnos kai nyn kai eis pantas tous aiōnas amēn: 'to the only God our Savior through Jesus Christ our Lord, be glory, majesty, power, and authority before all ages, now and for all ages.' The doxology is structurally unique in the NT: four attributes (doxa/glory, megalōsynē/majesty, kratos/power, exousia/authority) are ascribed with a triple time-span (before all ages, now, forever). Megalōsynē (majesty, greatness) — used in the LXX for the divine majesty (Deuteronomy 32:3; Psalms 145:3) and in Hebrews 1:3, 8:1 for the exalted Son's session at the right hand. The four-attribute formula echoes Daniel's throne-room language (Daniel 7:14: glory, honor, dominion — to the Son of Man) and 1 Chronicles 29:11 (yours, O LORD, is greatness, power, glory, victory, and majesty). The ascription is dia Iēsou Christou (through Jesus Christ) — making Christ the channel of all glory given to God.
